eli3: Chunking is the process of splitting large documents into smaller pieces before storing them in a vector database. You can't embed an entire 100-page PDF as one unit — the embedding would be too general to be useful for specific questions. Chunks are the units of retrieval.

Chunk size matters a lot. Too small (50 tokens): each chunk is a sentence, highly precise but missing context when retrieved. Too large (2000 tokens): each chunk is several paragraphs, lots of context but retrieval is imprecise (many irrelevant sentences dilute the signal). Most systems use 256–512 token chunks with some overlap between adjacent chunks.

Modern chunking strategies go beyond simple fixed-size splitting: semantic chunking splits at topic boundaries, parent-child chunking stores small and large versions of the same content, and late chunking uses modern embedding models that chunk and embed in a single pass.

whenWhy.title: Embedding quality degrades with long texts
whenWhy.body: Embedding models (text-embedding-3-small, BGE) are trained on short passages (typically < 512 tokens). When you embed longer texts, the model averages information across all tokens — the embedding becomes a blurry summary that doesn't represent any specific detail well. Chunking at ~512 tokens aligns with the embedding model's training distribution, producing embeddings that capture specific facts and concepts. Retrieval precision is directly proportional to how well the chunk embedding captures what a relevant query would match.

whenWhy.title: Different queries need different granularity
whenWhy.body: A question about a specific number ('What was Apple's 2023 revenue?') is best answered by a small, precise chunk containing that sentence. A question requiring context ('Why did Apple's revenue decline in 2023?') needs a larger chunk with surrounding explanation. Advanced strategies address this: store both small chunks (for retrieval) and their parent paragraphs (for generation). Retrieve small chunks, return parent context. This 'small-to-big' strategy gives retrieval precision and generation context.

whenWhy.title: Boundary effects cause retrieval failures
whenWhy.body: Naive fixed-size chunking splits sentences in the middle, separates a claim from its evidence, or cuts a table row from its header. These boundary errors cause the retrieved chunk to be incomplete or misleading. Chunk overlap (50-100 token overlap between adjacent chunks) partially mitigates this — a sentence cut at a boundary appears in both adjacent chunks. Semantic chunking (split at paragraph/section boundaries, not at fixed token counts) eliminates most boundary issues but is more complex to implement.

deepDiveIntro: Chunking is the foundational, most consequential decision in RAG pipeline design — yet it is often treated as a trivial preprocessing step. The core tension is structural: embedding models have token-length limits (typically 512 tokens for models like BGE-base and text-embedding-3-small) and are trained on short, topically focused passages. Embedding a full multi-page document violates that training distribution, producing a vector that is a blurry average over all the document's topics and facts. Any specific detail — a revenue figure, a technical specification, a named entity — becomes statistically diluted, making it impossible to retrieve that chunk reliably when a user poses a precise factual question. The solution is to split documents into passages short enough to be densely represented by the embedding model, yet long enough to carry sufficient context for the LLM to synthesise a complete answer.

The fundamental trade-off in chunking is between retrieval precision and semantic completeness. Short chunks (128–256 tokens) produce embeddings that are tightly focused on a single idea or fact, maximising cosine similarity between the chunk and a matching query — but the retrieved snippet may lack the surrounding context needed to formulate a coherent answer. Long chunks (1024+ tokens) carry ample context but dilute the embedding signal, causing the chunk to rank poorly against specific queries even when it contains the correct answer. Advanced strategies such as parent-child chunking and sentence-window retrieval resolve this tension by decoupling the unit of retrieval (small, precise child chunk) from the unit of synthesis (large, context-rich parent chunk). Empirical evaluation on a representative query set is the only reliable way to identify the optimal strategy for a given document collection. [1]

deepDive.heading: Fixed-Size vs Semantic Chunking
deepDive.body: Fixed-size: split every N tokens (or characters) with overlap M. Simple, fast, no ML required. Default: 512 tokens, 50-token overlap. Weakness: splits mid-sentence, mid-table, mid-list. Best for: homogeneous, well-structured documents (technical manuals, encyclopedia articles). Semantic: use an embedding model to detect topic shifts — split when the cosine similarity between adjacent sentence embeddings drops below a threshold. Produces chunks that are semantically coherent but variable in length. Slower (requires embedding every sentence during indexing). Best for: heterogeneous documents with multiple topics (news articles, research papers). LangChain SemanticChunker implements this with three breakpoint threshold types: percentile, standard_deviation, interquartile. [1]

deepDive.heading: Parent-Child and Sentence-Window Chunking
deepDive.body: Parent-child (hierarchical) chunking: index two sizes simultaneously. Child chunks: 128 tokens (high precision for retrieval). Parent chunks: 512 tokens (sufficient context for synthesis). At retrieval time: search child chunk embeddings (small = precise match). At synthesis time: return the parent chunk (large = full context). Implementation: store parent_id in child chunk metadata; after retrieving child chunks, fetch their parents. LlamaIndex AutoMergingRetriever does this automatically. Sentence-window: embed individual sentences; at retrieval, return the sentence ± K surrounding sentences (window_size=3 means return 7 sentences total). More fine-grained than parent-child. [1]

deepDive.heading: Late Chunking with Long-Context Embedding Models
deepDive.body: Late chunking (Jina AI, 2024): a novel approach that avoids the traditional chunking → embedding pipeline. Feed the entire document to a long-context embedding model (jina-embeddings-v2, max 8192 tokens). The model produces one embedding per token (contextual token embeddings). Then pool token embeddings into chunk embeddings by mean-pooling the token embeddings within each chunk boundary. Result: each chunk embedding is contextualised by the full document, not just the chunk itself. This preserves cross-chunk context (e.g., a pronoun in chunk 3 that refers to a noun in chunk 1 is correctly contextualised). Demonstrated to outperform standard chunking on multi-hop retrieval benchmarks. [2]

deepDive.heading: Late Chunking and ColBERT-style Token-level Retrieval
deepDive.body: ColBERT (Khattab & Zaharia 2020) pushes token-level representation further than late chunking: rather than pooling token embeddings into a single chunk vector, ColBERT stores every token embedding from every passage at index time (Multi-Vector representation). At query time, each query token embedding is independently matched against all passage token embeddings; relevance is computed as the sum of maximum cosine similarities across query tokens (MaxSim operator). This late interaction mechanism is far more expressive than a single dot product between compressed bi-encoder embeddings and captures fine-grained lexical alignment. The trade-off is storage: a 512-token passage with 768-dimensional embeddings requires 512 × 768 × 4 bytes ≈ 1.5 MB per passage vs. ~3 KB for a single-vector bi-encoder. RAGatouille (Python library) wraps ColBERT v2 for plug-in use in LangChain/LlamaIndex pipelines. ColBERT consistently outperforms single-vector retrieval on BEIR benchmarks, particularly for multi-hop and compositional queries. [2]

deepDive.heading: Late Chunking and ColBERT-style Retrieval
deepDive.body: Standard RAG pipelines chunk text first, then embed each chunk independently — a process called early chunking. The problem: when a sentence in chunk 3 contains a pronoun referring to an entity introduced in chunk 1, the chunk-3 embedding has no knowledge of that entity, producing a semantically impoverished vector. Late chunking (Jina AI, 2024) reverses this order: feed the full document to a long-context embedding model (e.g., jina-embeddings-v2, max 8192 tokens), obtain one contextual embedding per token via the model's full self-attention, then mean-pool token embeddings within each desired chunk boundary. Each resulting chunk embedding is contextualised by the entire document — cross-chunk references are preserved in the vector. Compared to early chunking on multi-hop retrieval benchmarks, late chunking improves recall by 5-10%. ColBERT takes the multi-vector idea further: instead of pooling, store every individual token embedding for every passage (late interaction). At query time, compute relevance as the sum of maximum cosine similarities between each query token embedding and any passage token embedding (MaxSim). This per-token matching is significantly more expressive than a single dot product but requires proportionally more storage (≈ 1.5 MB per 512-token passage at 768d vs. ~3 KB for a single-vector bi-encoder). RAGatouille wraps ColBERT v2 for direct integration in LangChain and LlamaIndex. [2]

deepDive.heading: Metadata-Enriched Chunking
deepDive.body: Attaching rich metadata to every chunk at index time is one of the highest-leverage, lowest-cost improvements to a RAG pipeline. Useful metadata fields: section_heading (the H1/H2 heading under which the chunk falls), document_title, page_number, date (document creation or last-modified date), author, document_type (contract, report, FAQ, code), and source_url. In LangChain, metadata is a dict attached to the Document object: `Document(page_content=chunk_text, metadata={"title": "Q3 2024 Report", "section": "Revenue", "date": "2024-09-30", "page": 12})`. At retrieval time, metadata filters narrow the search space before ANN search runs — dramatically improving precision for scoped queries: `retriever.get_relevant_documents(query, filter={"date": {"$gte": "2024-01-01"}, "document_type": "report"})`. For unstructured documents where metadata is not available in the file system, use an LLM to extract metadata at ingestion time: prompt the LLM with the first 1000 tokens of the document and ask it to extract title, author, date, and topic tags. This LLM-based metadata extraction adds ~$0.001 per document but unlocks structured filtering across an otherwise unstructured corpus. Section headings are especially valuable: prepending the section heading to each chunk's text ("Section: Revenue Analysis — [chunk text]") also improves embedding quality for section-specific queries.

codeSnapshot.label: rag_chunking.py
codeSnapshot.code:
from langchain_core.documents import Document
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

# --- Sample documents ---
docs = [
    Document(page_content="LangChain supports multiple chunking strategies for RAG pipelines. "
             "Fixed-size chunking is the simplest approach. Semantic chunking detects topic shifts. "
             "Parent-child chunking stores both small and large versions for retrieval and synthesis."),
    Document(page_content="OpenAI embedding models convert text to high-dimensional vectors. "
             "These vectors capture semantic meaning and enable similarity search."),
]

# ------------------------------------------------------------------
# Strategy 1: Fixed-size chunking (RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter)
# ------------------------------------------------------------------
fixed_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=512,
    chunk_overlap=50,
    length_function=len,
)
fixed_chunks = fixed_splitter.split_documents(docs)
print(f"Fixed-size chunks: {len(fixed_chunks)}")
for c in fixed_chunks:
    print(f"  [{len(c.page_content)} chars] {c.page_content[:80]}...")

# ------------------------------------------------------------------
# Strategy 2: Semantic chunking (splits at topic-shift boundaries)
# ------------------------------------------------------------------
from langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
semantic_splitter = SemanticChunker(
    embeddings,
    breakpoint_threshold_type="percentile",  # options: percentile, standard_deviation, interquartile
)
semantic_chunks = semantic_splitter.split_documents(docs)
print(f"\nSemantic chunks: {len(semantic_chunks)}")
for c in semantic_chunks:
    print(f"  [{len(c.page_content)} chars] {c.page_content[:80]}...")

# ------------------------------------------------------------------
# Strategy 3: Parent-child chunking (ParentDocumentRetriever)
# Small child chunks for retrieval; large parent chunks returned at synthesis time.
# ------------------------------------------------------------------
from langchain.retrievers import ParentDocumentRetriever
from langchain.storage import InMemoryStore
from langchain_chroma import Chroma

# Child splitter: small chunks used for embedding and retrieval
child_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=128, chunk_overlap=20)
# Parent splitter: larger chunks returned to the LLM after retrieval
parent_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=50)

vectorstore = Chroma(
    collection_name="parent_child_demo",
    embedding_function=embeddings,
)
doc_store = InMemoryStore()  # stores parent chunks keyed by ID

retriever = ParentDocumentRetriever(
    vectorstore=vectorstore,
    docstore=doc_store,
    child_splitter=child_splitter,
    parent_splitter=parent_splitter,
)

# Index documents (adds both parent and child chunks)
retriever.add_documents(docs)

# Retrieve: searches child embeddings, returns parent chunks
results = retriever.get_relevant_documents("What chunking strategies does LangChain support?")
print(f"\nParent-child retrieval results: {len(results)}")
for r in results:
    print(f"  [{len(r.page_content)} chars] {r.page_content[:100]}...")

takeaways: Chunk size trade-off: small = precise retrieval, less context; large = more context, imprecise retrieval. Default: 512 tokens. [1]
Chunk overlap (50-100 tokens) mitigates boundary splits — critical for sentence-spanning facts. [1]
Parent-child: embed small child chunks (retrieve precisely), return large parent chunks (synthesise with context). [1]
Semantic chunking: split on embedding similarity drops — coherent topics, variable length, slower indexing. [1]
Late chunking: contextualised chunk embeddings from long-context models — better cross-chunk retrieval. [2]

edgeCases.q: How do you chunk structured data like tables and code files?
edgeCases.a: Tables: never split a table across chunks — extract each table as a complete unit (use pdfplumber or Camelot for PDFs). Embed the table as Markdown (preserves structure). If a table is very large (> 1000 tokens), split by row groups with the header row repeated in each chunk. Alternative: convert the table to natural language ('Row 1: Q1 2024 revenue was $5.2B, representing 12% YoY growth'). Code: split at function/class boundaries, not at character count. Use AST parsing (Python's ast module, tree-sitter for multi-language) to identify function definitions; each function is one chunk. Never split inside a function. Add metadata: {file_path, function_name, class_name, language}. For code RAG, code-specific embedding models (CodeBERT, code-embedding-3-small) outperform text embedding models.

edgeCases.q: How do you determine the optimal chunk size experimentally?
edgeCases.a: Systematic evaluation: (1) Create a golden dataset: 50-100 question-answer pairs over your documents where you know which passage contains the answer; (2) Test chunk sizes: 128, 256, 512, 1024 tokens; for each size, build the index and run all questions; (3) Metrics: retrieval hit rate (does the correct chunk appear in top-k retrieved results?), MRR (mean reciprocal rank of the correct chunk), and end-to-end answer accuracy; (4) Pick the chunk size that maximises the combination of hit rate and end-to-end accuracy. Typical finding: 256-512 is optimal for most document types; very long-form documents (textbooks) benefit from 512-1024. The evaluation cost is low (a few hundred LLM calls) but prevents weeks of debugging production retrieval failures.
